title: Executive Order 9484—Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants and Facilities of the Farrell Cheek Steel Company, Located at Sandusky, Ohio

flags.justification: Broad seizure and operating authority ('any manner that he deems necessary'), though narrowed in practice by the requirement to observe specific, already-issued War Labor Board directive orders (Sec. 2).

Sec. 4 sets a concrete 60-day cap, and Sec. 2 requires a report to the President if net operating income proves insufficient to cover accrued wage increases — a specific, textually explicit reporting obligation.
Single purpose: seizure of this one company's plants to resolve an ongoing labor disturbance.
Sec. 2 requires observing specific, named War Labor Board directive orders (with exact dates), a concrete operating constraint rather than open discretion.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
Sec. 4's 60-day cap after restored productive efficiency is a genuine, explicit termination trigger.
No judicial-process-blocking or similar oversight-removal language is present.
The 60-day cap, the requirement to follow specific pre-existing War Labor Board determinations, and the reporting-to-President obligation together provide multiple real checks.
A routine application of the established wartime plant-seizure template.
No other agency's mandate is implicated; this order does not include a management-preservation or multi-agency cooperation clause, but neither creates any conflict.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 1/20 = 5.0%. Notable for the reporting-to-President obligation on wage shortfalls — a real, specific accountability feature not present in several sibling seizure orders.
